data.title: PayPal Holdings: Fundamentals And Innovation Drive Value
data.description:
At this time, in this market, I believe that one of the most attractive investment opportunities is payment processing behemoth PayPal Holdings (PYPL). Shares of the company are attractively priced and, while financial performance has been admittedly mixed, the overall trend for the firm has been positive. Management continues to make interesting moves aimed at keeping the company an industry leader. And at the end of the day, I firmly believe that shareholders will benefit from this combination of factors.

This was my general thinking when, in March of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. Leading up to that point, shares had fallen pretty significantly. This was in spite of the fact that management was demonstrating the ability for the company to grow moving forward. Since then, the stock has risen by about 6.9%. But considering that the S&P 500 is up 6.6% over that same window of time, I don't see this as a monumental win just yet. For those focused on the long haul, however, I firmly believe that this opportunity will pay off. As such, I will keep it rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

A Great Business At A Great Price

if we focus purely on the fundamental argument, it is difficult to view PayPal in anything other than a positive light. Take the most recent data provided by management as an example. This data covers the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $7.79 billion. That represented an increase of 1.2% compared to the $7.70 billion generated one year earlier. I know that's not a significant amount of growth. But its growth all the same.

During the quarter, the company boasted 436 million active accounts on its platform. That was comfortably above the 427 million reported one year earlier and compared favorably to the 434 million the business ended 2024 with. This is not to say that everything was great. Interestingly, while there were more active accounts, the company actually experienced a decline in the number of payment transactions from 6.51 billion to 6.05 billion. This was driven by a reduction in the number of transactions per active account from 60 to 59.4.

A big pain point for the company here involved its PSP (Payment Service Provider), which is the firm's service dedicated to merchants that empowers them to accept payments both online and in-person. The drop that the company saw here seems to have been a deliberate choice, driven by management’s decision to move away from unprofitable transaction volume. If we exclude PSP figures from the equation, the total number of transactions would have jumped from 3.60 billion to 3.81 billion, while the transactions per account rose from 33.9 to 35.2.

It is worth pointing out that total payment volume for the company also grew year over year, climbing from $403.86 billion to $417.21 billion. This expansion was fueled in large part by the company’s wildly successful Venmo platform. From the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025, total payment volume for Venmo expanded a hefty 9.6% from $69.27 billion to $75.94 billion. The company is seeing other successes as well. One example would be its branded checkout feature, which provides merchants with their own styled card forms and buttons, and also grants some more control over a variety of other features and experiences as part of the payment collection and processing side of things. If we remove the leap day effect, then branded checkout saw growth of about 6% year over year. Management attributed this to strength across large enterprise platforms and marketplaces.

The growth in revenue for the company has brought with it a rise in profitability as well. Net income actually shot up 44.9% from $888 million to $1.29 billion. A big driver of this improvement was a reduction in restructuring and other expenses from $212 million to only $66 million. However, the company saw other reductions as well. As an example, transaction expenses declined from 50.88% of revenue to 47.54%. The move away from unprofitable volume, as well as favorable changes in merchant mix, helped make this possible. On top of this, the company also saw a reduction in customer support and operations costs from 5.90% of revenue to 5.11%. $40 million of the $56 million decline was thanks to a reduction in headcount and lower software expenses. It is worth mentioning that, if we adjust for certain one time items, net profits still rose solidly from $1.16 billion to $1.33 billion.

These changes are solid proof that while management is still focused on some key growth initiatives, a lot of the extra value that should accrue to investors will come from optimizing existing business operations. The only profitability metric that worsened year over year was operating cash flow. It fell from $1.92 billion to $1.16 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a pop from $1.80 billion to $1.84 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.61 billion to $1.81 billion.

In addition to management making significant improvements in profits and cash flows, there is the expectation that the rest of this year will look pretty solid. Management anticipates, for instance, adjusted earnings per share of between $4.95 and $5.10. Given the current share count outstanding, and taking the midpoint of this range, this would represent an increase of 3.8% compared to the $4.84 billion generated in 2024. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would also get adjusted operating cash flow of $8.16 billion and EBITDA of $6.96 billion.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. I also included valuation figures using historical results from last year. For a high-quality industry leader that generates meaningful cash flows, and that should continue growing in the long run, these are multiples that I consider to be quite appealing. Furthermore, management continues to reward shareholders directly. In the first quarter of this year, the company repurchased $1.50 billion worth of shares. That worked out to roughly 19 million shares. The company ended the quarter with $18.4 billion remaining under its share buyback programs. And in all likelihood, they would continue buying back stock as time goes on. Shares are also attractively priced compared to other similar enterprises. In the table below, I compared it to five such firms. Using each of the three valuation metrics, we can see that only one of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
PayPal Holdings (PYPL) 14.3 8.7 11.3
Automatic Data Processing (ADP) 33.4 27.8 21.5
Fiserv (FI) 29.2 14.7 13.0
Fidelity National Information Services (FIS) 53.0 18.1 15.1
Global Payments (GPN) 12.3 5.4 7.7
Paychex (PAYX) 33.2 32.4 22.7

At the start of this article, I stressed that management continues to make interesting moves aimed at creating shareholder value in the long run. There's plenty of evidence to back this claim up. Just the other day, on June 4th, the firm announced that it was launching both the Venmo Debit Card and Venmo Checkout. Venmo is used by 64 million US account holders. And, at this time, it is growing like a weed. During the first quarter of this year, revenue was 20% higher than it was the previous year. The Pay with Venmo functionality had seen total payment volume up over 50% year over year, while the number of monthly active accounts were up around 30%.

Using the Venmo Debit Card, users are able to use tap to pay. They can automatically add funds to their accounts and even use the feature for international purchases. There's also the ability to use it with online purchases at checkout and the program is even pushing a new brand campaign aimed at furthering adoption. Another investment that management is making involves providing additional cash back offers that include as much as 15% cash back for certain purchases. That is a massive amount of cash back compared to credit cards.

Also this month, the company announced the launch of its new PayPal Credit card that can be used for both online and in person purchases. And what's really exciting here is that customers can continue to get the special financing on all purchases over $149. This is a feature that I use regularly. You get 0% financing on purchases over the aforementioned amount, so long as you pay off the balance in full within six months. I use this for my guilty pleasure of buying chess sets and chess clocks.

Management is also making interesting moves when it comes to the cryptocurrency space. Although this is not an industry that I believe in the potential of, it clearly represents a good opportunity for payment processing firms. In late April, management announced that it had expanded its partnership with Coinbase (COIN) in order to give that platform’s customers direct access to PayPal’s PayPal USD stablecoin. In essence, customers at Coinbase would be able to make transactions with and even trade PYUSD without incurring any platform fees and while being able to redeem PYUSD on a one-for-one basis. They are also exploring certain use cases together in the DeFi space. This could create meaningful value for shareholders in the long run.

Takeaway

At this time, I think that PayPal is one of the most attractive opportunities on the market. Shares of the business are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. Management continues to make operational improvements and is investing in interesting growth initiatives. On top of this, the company is buying back stock in large amounts. Add all of this together, and I firmly believe that this company easily deserves the ‘strong buy’ rating I have had for a while now.

data.title: JD.com: Buybacks, Dividends, And Discounted Multiples
data.description:
Over the past few years, JD.com (JD) has evolved from a basic online retailer into one of China’s leading e-commerce players. Today it is a technological ecosystem, with a focus on strict quality control and logistics – JD was the first to follow the model of building its network of warehouses and couriers, and now the platform is able to deliver goods in a day. This distinguishes the company from Alibaba, which only acts as an intermediary between the seller and the buyer.

In 2023-2025, JD went through a transformation stage focusing on margin growth, in fact, sacrificing high-growth rates – roughly speaking, it moved from a growth company to maturity. The company started paying dividends, strengthened its discount program, launched food delivery, and supported manufacturers affected by the tariff war with the United States. At the same time, three key segments — JD Retail, JD Logistics and New Businesses — are developing in different ways and each has its own role in the ecosystem: Retail brings profit and scale, Logistics provides an infrastructural advantage, and New Businesses forms the foundation for the future. Today, JD is not just a seller, but a full—fledged infrastructure player who is building the e-commerce of the future, flexibly adapting to the challenges of the time.

Competing On Price, Defending On Quality

Despite all the transformations, the basis of JD's business remains traditional e-commerce (up to 80% of all revenue) — the company independently buys and sells goods, focusing primarily on electronics, household appliances and products. However, despite the large-scale turnover, this area is characterized by low margins due to high cost and competition, which puts pressure on prices. The share of service revenue is gradually growing — marketplace, advertising, logistics — where JD helps to develop sales on its platform to other sellers. The management strategy is to increase the share of service income in total revenue, so the margin is higher.

Together with Alibaba and Pinduoduo, the company controls up to 80% of China's online sales, but each of the players occupies its own niche: JD is a leader in electronics and quality logistics, PDD is an aggressive discounter, Alibaba is an ecosystem with maximum coverage. At the same time, JD is losing ground in the mass segment: PDD has lost ground in terms of the number of customers, and in the low-cost segment it has to spend billions on discounts and marketing to keep the audience's attention.

Trump's return and new tariffs on Chinese imports are hitting, albeit indirectly, but noticeably. JD is not an exporter, it operates in China, but if the entire economy slows down, producers' incomes fall, and consumers start counting every yuan. To somehow support demand and exporters, JD launched purchases for 200 billion yuan — a good idea, it will increase the range, give low prices. But at the same time, this will further increase competition among sellers. And if the yuan falls, imports will rise in price — this is immediately a minus for JD Worldwide. Altogether, the tariffs, the exchange rate, and the general slowdown in the market can hit JD.

Operating Metrics Improve, But EPS Risks Linger

In the first quarter of 2025 JD.com It showed impressive revenue growth of $41.5 billion, which is 15.8% higher than last year. The key drivers were the recovery of consumer demand, a sound pricing strategy and the expansion of the customer base: the number of active JD users increased by more than 20% year-on-year. The growth was based on sales of electronics (+17%) and consumer goods (+15%), which indicates an increase in confidence in JD as a universal trading platform. The flagship segment of JD Retail grew by 16%, logistics — by 11.5%, and New Businesses, for the first time in a long time, showed growth of 18% due to consolidation of food delivery and accelerated services.

JD's operating margin showed good results this quarter — it reached 3.5%, and this is plus 37% compared to last year's profit. If you look at non-GAAP, there is generally 3.9%, which indicates that the business inside is becoming more stable and efficient. JD Retail not only increased revenue, but also squeezed more out of it due to the growth of service areas and careful cost management. In fact, the retail platform is already operating as an adult business — without kinks and with clear logic. Net profit immediately increased by 53% to 10.9 billion yuan, and EPS grew by 58%. Profits from shares in associated companies have made an important contribution here.

Management sees signs of a steady recovery in consumption, expecting revenue growth in the range of 10-15%. The company expects to maintain a non-GAAP margin of 3.5–4%, although part of the revenue will go to investments such as food delivery, subsidies, and logistics expansion. The question is not revenue, but net profit: the consensus of analysts assumes its decline in 2025, despite record figures in the first quarter. The reason is the expectation of increased competition and investment pressure. At the same time, the consensus also expects revenue growth of 12-13%.

DCF And Multiples Suggest Asymmetric Risk/Reward

In my model, I proceed from a simple but important premise: JD.com it will grow, but you will have to pay for this growth. I predict that revenue will increase by an average of 6% per year, from $158.8 billion to $226.8 billion by 2030. This is quite realistic, given the recovery in demand and the expansion of services. But at the same time, the operating margin will remain under pressure. In 2025, I expect operating profit to drop to $3.5 billion — the company will obviously spend on new directions and the struggle for the price. The situation will improve slightly by 2030, with an EBIT forecast of $5.1 billion. EBITDA will follow approximately the same path: $4.8 billion in 2025 and $7 billion closer to 2029. CapEx will remain within 1-2% of revenue, as it has been historically. As a result, it turns out that with such introductory data, JD can give an FCF yield of 1-2%, as it has been in the last 5 years, when the average FCF yield was at 2%.

According to my calculations, the WACC for JD came out at 4.26%. Almost 80% of the capital structure is occupied by cost of equity, which means that management primarily relies on its own capital in raising capital. The cost of equity calculations turned out to be 4.61% — here the main drivers were the risk premium of 5.3% and the base rate of 1.7% on Chinese 10-year government bonds. The low cost of equity was also affected by the low beta at 0.43x. I use the latest TTM Interest Expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt: before taxes, the rate was 3.3%, and after the tax shield — about 2.9%, which is logical under current market conditions and the balance sheet structure (debt occupies about 20% of capital). To estimate the terminal cost, I did not rely on one method — I took several at once: I used the EV/EBITDA and P/E multipliers and supplemented this with the perpetuity growth model. This gives a more balanced picture and reduces the bias that can occur if you focus on only one approach.

JD.com It is currently trading at a significant discount to all key multipliers — securities are 1.5–2 times cheaper than the sector in terms of P/E, EV/EBITDA and EV/Sales, and in comparison with their own history they look even more undervalued. Non-GAAP TTM P/E is only 7.15x versus 30+ on average over five years, EV/EBITDA is 4.83x versus historical 29.63x, and EV/Sales fell to 0.21x, which is almost six times lower than the market average. All this indicates a clear underestimation of the company's shares, and if the company continues to show stable results, this discount may well begin to narrow.

The consensus expects that JD's P/E will remain at a low level in the coming years: almost 8x in 2025, it gradually decreases to 6.10x by 2027, so I used a P/E multiplier of about 7x to evaluate the stock in the baseline scenario.

Judging by the valuation graph using the P/E model, with the current share price around $34, the potential depends on which parameters we take as the base. If you use an 8x multiplier and a 4.3% discount rate, then the fair price is the same as the current one — there is almost no potential. With a slightly more optimistic approach (P/E 9x and a rate of 3.3%), the upside reaches $40, which gives an increase of almost 19%. But if the market evaluates JD more conservatively — say, with a multiplier of 7x and a rate of 5.3% — then the fair price drops to $31, that is, the downside is about 8%. So the range of possible revaluation is quite wide: from -8% to +19%, and much depends on the assessment of the risk premium and business profitability expectations.

If you look at the current valuation through EV/EBITDA, then with prices around $34, you can see that the shares are still undervalued. With a 5x multiplier and a 4.3% discount rate, the fair value is closer to $46, which gives an upside of 21.6%. Even with more conservative parameters — for example, a 5.3% discount and 4x EV/EBITDA — the shares still look attractive, with a fair price being 7.1% higher than the current one. On the contrary, in a more "bullish" scenario — the rate is 3.3% and the multiplier is 6x — the fair price goes beyond $ 46, which opens an upside of over 37%. Overall, JD's EV/EBITDA doesn't look too undervalued in all scenarios.

In the basic scenario of the valuation using the perpetuity growth model — with a WACC of 4.3% and constant growth of 2.5% — the fair value of the shares reaches an upside of about 13%. If we take more conservative inputs, for example, an increase of only 2% and a discount of 5.3%, the estimate drops sharply to $33, which means a potential decline of 2.4%. At the same time, in positive scenarios — WACC 3.3% and growth of 3% — the fair price reaches $42, which gives an upside of about 11%. The spread of estimates between the scenarios — almost $10 per share, or about 25% of the current price — highlights how important it is to evaluate using different methods.

To get a more balanced final estimate, I combined three approaches — DCF with perpetuity growth, P/E and EV/EBITDA estimates — into one model with equal weights. In the basic scenario, which uses neutral parameters for multipliers and the discount rate, the average fair price is about $38, with an upside of 13%. In a negative case, the valuation drops to $33 (downside of only 1%), and in a positive case, it rises to $43, which gives an upside of about 27%. Thus, the range of estimates is quite narrow — from -1% to +27%, and this creates a sense of restrained risk. Against this background, taking into account the high underestimation of the company by multipliers both historically and in comparison with industry averages, I assign the company a "Buy" rating.

Capital Allocation Shift: JD Embraces Distributions

Over the past couple of years, the company has dramatically changed its approach to capital recovery. Previously, the company almost did not share profits, invested everything in growth, and now it has become one of the most active in the sector in terms of payments. It all started with a one—time dividend in 2022, then regular payments began: $0.62 for ADS in 2023, $1.00 in 2025 – this is about 2.5-3% return on the current price. Buybacks are also present. In 2024 and Q1 2025 alone, JD repurchased $5.1 billion worth of shares, which reduced the circulation of securities by more than 8%. And at the same time, the profit for 2024 was $5.7 billion, and there is still more than $20 billion in cash in the accounts.

JD buys stocks at drawdown times, not on a schedule. A new program for another $5 billion has been launched until 2027, and $1.5 billion has already been spent. The combination of regular dividends and active cashback shows that the company is no longer just building a business, but is quietly sharing cash flow with shareholders. This is a transition from the "grow by all means" mode to a stage where growth and payments to shareholders run in parallel. Especially considering how conservative Chinese public companies used to be in this regard, JD is becoming particularly interesting for global investors.

Risks

The competition with Alibaba, Pinduoduo and others is getting tougher, especially considering that JD focuses on low-margin products, where the price war is strongest. At the same time, attempts to go beyond e-commerce are still unprofitable: food delivery, international markets — all this generates losses and pulls the profits of the underlying business down. Both inside China, from regulators, and outside, pressure is exerted on the company's business due to the United States, tariffs, and the risk of delisting. JD is very dependent on the Chinese economy: if consumers tighten their belts or the government dramatically changes the rules, this immediately affects profit forecasts.

Final Thoughts

JD.com It used to be a typical "growth action" — we invest everything in development, forget about returning capital to shareholders. Right now, the focus is completely different: efficiency, cash for shareholders, and holding positions in a still rather turbulent market. Yes, the transformation is already noticeable, but the competitive environment is still challenging, and China's macro and regulatory environment can easily confuse even the most resilient. At the same time, stocks look cheap both in terms of multipliers and in comparison with history, and the valuation model through DCF and multipliers gives a fairly even picture: a 10-15% growth potential. I think that JD will be able to maintain its margin and not fail in terms of growth, so I assign the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Looming Conflict
data.description:
The most recent number for the M2 money stock was right around $21.9 trillion.

The previous peak level for the M2 money stock was right around $21.7 trillion.

Looking at the chart for the M2 money stock, in the past three years, from April 2022 to April 2025, the M2 money stock has been roughly flat.

Yes, the M2 money stock declined through the first half of this time period... modestly... and then rose for the remainder of this period.

Analysts were predicting that the U.S. economy would face a recession sometime during this time span, but an economic recession never occurred.

During this period, I took a longer look at the scene and argued that from April 2020 through April 2022, the M2 money stock rose by about $6.5 trillion, and this money was hanging around the banking system and was just waiting to be used.

It is still waiting to be used.

The velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock dropped from about 1.390 in the first quarter of 2020 to around 1.128 in the first quarter of 2022 and has only recently risen to about 1.320.

People did not use the money that had been pumped into the economy during the economic turmoil of the early 2020s...and have not really returned to spending patterns that existed previous to the 2020s.

In other words, over the previous five years, the spending behavior of individuals changed, and the result was to modify any potential inflation that might have resulted from the excessive Federal Reserve monetary action in the early twenties.

The thing that worries the Federal Reserve at this time is that people could begin to use these monies more intensively and generate a period of higher inflation...just from all the excess cash lying around in bank vaults right now.

The compound rate of growth of the M2 money stock during the first half of the 2020s has been around 8.5 percent. This is definitely an inflationary amount of money creation.

Inflation could still explode at any time.

Donald Trump seems to want higher rates of inflation, a signal that the economy was expanding at a more rapid rate...a signal of his "golden era."

And, this is exactly that thing that Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell wants to avoid.

And, the discussion seems to be picking up that Mr. Trump is going to start putting more and more pressure on Mr. Powell to ease up on the Fed's current round of "quantitative tightening."

The Fed's position of quantitative tightening has been going on for over three years.

Here is the chart showing what has happened to the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright.

As can be seen, the decline in the curve seems to have become more modest in recent months.

My interpretation of what the Federal Reserve is doing is that the Fed is now in its third round of quantitative tightening. That is, the Fed conducted two rounds of quantitative tightening before it ended in the current round, which I have claimed began in the banking week of September 18, 2024.

Since the banking week of September 16, 2024, the Federal Reserve has reduced the number of securities held outright by $315.2 billion. Let it be noted that the Fed also allowed loans to banks to run off in the amount of $92.9 billion. This means that Since the banking week of September 18, 2024, the Fed has overseen a decline of $428.1 billion in its lending to the commercial banking system.

Overall, the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve has declined by about $2.1 trillion since the quantitative tightening began.

So, the Federal Reserve has removed securities from its portfolio at a very slow and steady pace since it began quantitative tightening, but, as a consequence, has only removed a portion of the securities it acquired during the early years of the buildup.

The Fed still has a lot of securities to remove from its portfolio if it is to get back to a more normal size, one that is more consistent with the Fed running a stable, non-inflationary role in the U.S. economy.

And, this is what Fed Chairman Jerome Powell is fighting for against the current president.

As mentioned above, the battle may just be heating up.

data.title: Palantir: Don't Get Burned By The Hype
data.description:
My Thesis Update

I wrote my 1st article on Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) in January 2024 with a "Buy" rating, arguing at the time that its dip was a real gift. But as the stock price kept expanding, the risk/reward became limited (in my opinion), so I eventually downgraded PLTR to "Hold" in July 2024. Then I issued a "Sell" rating, and once PLTR finally fell and became more attractive, I upgraded it to "Hold" in mid-April this year, seeing the NATO deal as a game-changing factor. Since then, the stock has gone up by over 45%, strongly outperforming the broader market by a factor of over 3.

Despite the phenomenal strength of PLTR's price action, I think that its current stock price fully reflects the whole growth story of the firm in the next few years. It's a great company at an unreasonably expensive price, so it makes me reiterate my previous "Hold" again.

My Updated Reasoning

I admit that there's no need to counter the fact that Palantir is demonstrating phenomenal operational strength in recent quarters. In Q1 FY2025, for example, they managed to meet the EPS consensus of non-GAAP $0.13 (they actually exceeded the expectation by ~1.1%) and beat the revenue consensus estimate by 2.52%.

And the market reacted in a positive way: Since the Q1 came out, the stock price went up by ~9%, although the beat on the bottom line was one of the least (in terms of its magnitude) over the past few quarters.

Anyway, Palantir's business is really flourishing, especially in the U.S., where overall regional revenue went up by 55% YoY, bringing its share in the sales structure up to 71% as of Q1. The U.S. Commercial revenue skyrocketed by 71% YoY and surpassed $1 billion in ARR for the first time in its history, while the U.S. government revenue also went up, but by just 45% YoY. Now widely known AIP is going through a cycle of especially high demand, translating into substantial deal-making, with U.S. commercial TCV hitting $810 million in Q1, which is a 239% YoY increase on a dollar-weighted duration basis. And I think that TCV is quite sustainable because the number of deals worth $1 million or more doubled on a YoY basis in Q1, and given the "stickiness" of such clientele, I don't expect any deal breaks any time soon.

Based on the CTO's commentary on the call, Palantir's ontology and AIP should facilitate "enterprise autonomy," moving beyond copilots to "agents that make you 50 times more productive", so basically it positions PLTR to better translate LLM capabilities into tangible business outcomes compared to its rivals. Also, in my April article on the stock, the main topic of mine was Palantir's strategic significance providing its Maven Smart System to NATO for deployment across its 32 member states, which, I thought, was a real game changer for its non-US prospects. Looking at the rising budgets of NATO on defense, and also President Trump's proposed $175 billion "Golden Dome" global missile defense system, which has included Palantir as a potential key contributor, I think that catalyst is still in play and should contribute well to Palantir's top-line going forward.

The defence spending of the 23 EU countries that are NATO members amounted to almost 2% of their combined GDP in 2024 and is projected to reach 2.04% in 2025. Trump's re-election is intensifying the pressure on Europe's NATO members to raise their defence spending. The US President wants all NATO nations to increase defence spending to 5% of their GDP. This idea was backed by EU foreign affairs chief Kaja Kallas in January.

Source: Euronews

What was nice to see in PLTR's financials is that its Rule of 40 scored 83% - that's even more than the previous quarter's already solid 81%. In terms of cash flows, thanks to their non-GAAP EBIT margin now standing at 44%, which is 800 bps more than last year, and Palantir's adjusted FCF amounted to ~$370 million (~42% margin).

But here comes the part that I don't like about Palantir - it seems like everyone on the market is aware of their success and likely growth prospects amid those extremely bullish tailwinds - otherwise we'd not see the 31.2% CAGR priced-in for their EPS figure in the next 10 years:

And we already saw how a little misstep might risk an earnings miss at some point (in Q1, the beat was only 1.1%, so it looked like a statistical error).

By FY2028, Palantir's EPS should basically triple in size, but let's assume it will actually quadruple (instead of $1.49, let's assume we'll see $2.32 in FY2028 EPS). In this quite bullish case, PLTR would be valued at a 58.3x FY2028 P/E multiple. Let's also assume that Palantir somehow manages to grow its sales by 40% YoY from 2025 to 2028 (today's CAGR suggests 34%), and we'll see FY2028 sales at $10.7 billion. That would mean an implied P/S ratio of 29.8x instead of 33x at the time of this writing.

It's virtually impossible to compare PLTR to anything else on the stock market - I mean it's selling products and offerings, as well as its tech under the hood - but I think every business model should be judged by its prospective margins and also the firm's prospective revenue growth.

If we take high-growth and potentially high-margin tech company SoundHound (SOUN) or C3.ai (AI), we'll see their FY2027-28 P/S ratios at 13x and 4.6x, respectively, and as you can see, that's below PLTR's 29-30x even when we take into account the possibility of ongoing revenue and EPS beats. I see no need to reverse engineer a cash flow model here because even if we take PLTR's adjusted FCF and simply extrapolate to the next few years at premium growth rates in sales, we'll get a severe overvaluation, basically at any reasonable WACC. Another Seeking Alpha analyst, Dilantha De Silva, already showed it in his brilliant article a few days ago, and I generally agree with his takeaway.

Palantir is a great business, but I believe the current valuation is stretched to the extent that it represents more of a narrative-based momentum than the true economic reality facing the company. Although Palantir is likely to grow in double digits in the next few years, achieving a CAGR of close to 50% through the next 10 years (which is required to justify its valuation today) seems like a low probability outcome.

Your Takeaway

I don't want to lie, I like what I see in Palantir's business momentum and their mission to serve the U.S. military needs, as well as make AI streamline and improve their commercial clients' businesses. But I can't justify the cost investors incur purchasing PLTR this expensive because even at another layer of growth premium in addition to the currently excessive (in my view) consensus estimates. Honestly, if I had bought PLTR at $20-40/share, I'd be holding and trimming now, and that's why I understand those bulls who have owned the stock since its IPO. But I don't want to feed their bias, so I leave my "Hold" unchanged for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Gap: With Positive Comp Sales, This Is A Great Dip To Buy
data.description:
To me, with the S&P 500 retreating from highs near 6,000 on the slightest provocation, stocks appear very vulnerable with investors' recent flight into the safety of bonds. In my view, this is an expression of elevated valuations more than anything else, and as such, I continue to emphasize rotating more of my portfolio into value stocks.

Gap (GAP), in particular, looks quite enticing after the stock dropped sharply following the perception of weak Q2 guidance alongside its earnings release in late May. So far, the San Francisco-based retailer has showcased a remarkable return to comparable sales growth in all of its core brands save one. Alongside a recent dividend boost to $0.165 per quarter (yielding ~3.2% per year), I think Gap is quite a compelling buy:

I last wrote a bullish opinion on Gap in April, when the stock was trading around $19 per share. While it's true that I missed out on selling Gap on its brief spike to the high $20s before its recent earnings release, I remain encouraged by the progress Gap is making: not only on reviving comp sales in its core Gap and Old Navy brands, but also in bolstering its gross margin profile. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Gap.

Since the number-one reason for me to invest in Gap is its cheap valuation, let's start with a discussion of where its latest multiples are sitting against the company's guidance outlook.

Alongside its Q1 earnings release, Gap held its full-year FY25 outlook stable at 1-2% y/y sales growth and 8-10% y/y operating income growth (investors reacted more sharply to the fact that net sales are expected to be about flat in Q2, decelerating from Q1's low single digit growth).

The company has not provided an exact EPS range, in part due to uncertainty over tariffs. The company believes that it has about $250-$300 million of negative tariff impact in the second half of FY25, with a mitigation strategy in place for about half of this impact. Wall Street, meanwhile, has an EPS target of $2.21 for Gap. This puts Gap's P/E ratio at just 9.5x FY25 P/E.

To me, that cheap P/E is set against a number of strengths that, in my view, Gap is under-appreciated for. We like the fact that Gap has a number of brands catering to a wide range of price points: from the elevated Banana Republic to the more basics-oriented Old Navy, which is well-positioned to compete in the fast-fashion space against the likes of H&M and Uniqlo. Amid a tighter macroeconomic environment, Gap may actually benefit from more cost-conscious customers gravitating toward its cheaper brands for their apparel purchases - a trend that may actually be reflected in the company's recent comp sales expansion in its basic brands.

Investors should also be abundantly aware of the fact that Gap has a solid balance sheet, with $2.22 billion of cash against $1.49 billion of debt - or a $730 million net cash position that's worth ~9% of the company's current market value. When we include the impact of the company's generous net cash on its valuation, Gap's valuation is just 8.7x FY25 P/E on an ex-cash basis.

The company is putting this cash to good use, by funding its boosted dividend (which costs the company only $61 million per quarter, which is more than supportable by its current operating income trends not to mention its cash-rich balance sheet reserves). To me, there are plenty of reasons to remain long here: take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q1 Recap

Let's now go through Gap's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Gap's revenue grew 2.2% y/y to $3.46 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.42 billion (+0.8% y/y) by a sizable margin, while also accelerating from a -3.5% y/y growth rate in Q4 (which was unnaturally colored by a tough 14-week prior-year compare).

To me, the core measure of Gap's sales performance is in its comparable sales growth: which measures the revenue growth of each of Gap's core brands excluding the impact of newly built or recently closed store locations.

As shown in the chart above, Q1 comp sales clocked in at 2% y/y (only slightly weaker versus 3% in Q4) for the company as a whole. We note that Old Navy's growth at 3% y/y was flat to FY24, while Gap improved one point to 5% y/y growth, versus 4% y/y growth in FY24.

The company notes that a refreshed take on basic categories is helping drive new brand momentum for Gap. Per CEO Richard Dickson's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

This marks the brand's sixth consecutive quarter of positive comps and its eighth consecutive quarter of market share gains, clear indications that Gap is resonating with consumers and gaining relevance. Momentum in women's continued to build quarter-over-quarter, fueling the brand's strong Q1 performance. We are building a consistent brand narrative that we are applying with relentless repetition. In Q1, this was exemplified through our exciting Feels Like Gap campaign that leveraged music by Meta with a timely feature of Parker Posey. We continued to advance our authority in denim in Q1.

This is a foundational category for Gap that has been a key pillar of the brand's reinvigoration. We gained share in the category with on trend styles like wide leg, barrel, relax silhouettes and pull ons, all of which are exciting our customers. Taking insights from our Flatiron store, we're now rolling out an enhanced denim experience to top locations, a great example of how we're turning insights into action.

We also saw strength in key categories like fleece, sweaters, and sleepwear, essentials that are building deeper loyalty and driving brand affinity with our customer. The brand strategy including collaborations is attracting a new generation to Gap while reinforcing the brand to those who have loved us for years."

There are, of course, problem areas that partially offset these strengths. Banana Republic decelerated to flat y/y trends, after growing 1% y/y in FY24. We do expect further pressure on Banana Republic as consumer spending weakens, which may dampen sales for Gap's highest-priced brand. On the other hand, we may also see the brand buck consumer weakness thanks to its positioning as an accessible luxury. We note that Banana Republic represents just over 10% of the company's overall sales (Old Navy and Gap together are just about 80%).

Athleta, unfortunately, is the sore spot. We had hoped that Athleta would be able to pick up market share in a time when key higher-priced rivals like Nike (NKE) and Lululemon (LULU) are seeing sales weakness, especially in the U.S. market. It seems, however, that the impact of a tough economy plus heightened competition from startup yoga/athleisure brands is also eating away at Athleta's comp sales, down -8% in the quarter versus flat performance in FY24. The good news here is that Athleta is the smallest contributor to Gap's overall sales (less than 10% of revenue), and the company is aware of the issues and undergoing a brand reset for Athleta.

On the profitability front, we appreciate the fact that Gap's gross profit dollars rose 3.7% y/y to $1.49 billion. Gross margin improved 60bps y/y to 41.8%, a level that Gap expects to be able to maintain into Q2 and consistent with its goal of slight y/y gross margin expansion.

Tariffs are the major wildcard that will heavily impact 2H'25 results. The company notes that China is "less than 3%" of its sourcing by the end of FY25, while Indonesia and Vietnam collectively represented 46% in FY24. Based on 30% tariffs in place in China and 10% tariffs elsewhere, the company notes that it is facing up to $250-$300 million in raw impact, with a mitigation plan in place to eliminate roughly half of that burden. We note a ~$150 million hit on 2H'25 would mean a ~3 point reduction to gross margins (on ~$8 billion of 2H revenue, or roughly flat to last year). That being said, we do think these risks are already abundantly priced into the stock at a <10x P/E ratio.

Risks And Key Takeaways

The core message here is that while Gap is far from a perfect company, the stock is trading at attractive enough levels to handsomely compensate investors for bearing that risk. We should be mindful of several top-line risks (such as macro pressures bearing on Banana Republic, and Athleta's recent sharp declines in comp sales) as well as potential margin pressures from tariffs in the back half of the year.

At the same time, however, finding a stock that trades at a <10x P/E while also yielding 3%+ is rare in today's market, and it's a bargain I'm willing to take on.

data.title: Adobe: AI Presents Large Opportunities And Even Larger Challenges
data.description:
Adobe’s (ADBE) latest quarter shows a company still compounding double-digit revenue while parlaying Firefly generative AI into every corner of Creative Cloud, yet the very success of models like Google’s Veo 3 hints that creation itself is drifting toward commodity status. ADBE now trades at roughly 19 times forward earnings (cheaper than Autodesk’s 29 times and far below Microsoft’s 36 times) but its 8 times sales multiple still embeds a premium for an orchestration moat the market is no longer sure exists.

Intro

Adobe is pulling out all stops to prove that generative AI could expand its creative toll-road. The company's second-quarter revenue increase of 11% YoY appears to be clear evidence that Adobe still has a place in the age of AI. However, investors are still clearly skeptical as Adobe has fallen about 13% year-to-date against a backdrop of falling model costs and open-source experimentation. While core metrics remain healthy, Adobe's narrative faces the risk of frontier labs taking market share.

Creativity software historically benefitted from high switching costs; today those costs are psychological more than technical. Users who once stayed because “work already pays for my license” now see prompt-based tools that live in a browser tab. Adobe’s answer is deep workflow integration (embedding Firefly directly inside Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere, and Frame.io so that generative actions become another panel, not another app). The strategy is working so far, yet it raises a question central to this article: can Adobe remain command-and-control in a world where the act of creation itself is free and portable?

Thesis

Adobe occupies a liminal space where generative AI fortifies its creative toll-road even as frontier labs accelerate disruptive capabilities. Firefly’s embed inside Creative Cloud converts subscription inertia into AI stickiness, reassuring users with licensed training data and verifiable Content Credentials that safeguard commercial workflows. Early video features such as Generative Extend in Premiere show how Adobe translates raw models into utility, adding frames and ambient sound directly on the timeline rather than exporting to a separate tool. Yet, Google’s Veo 3 embodies an iteration pace that Adobe will find impossible to match. Enterprise clients are already lining up to generate videos using Veo 3. Such demand is not surprising as such a tool bypasses traditional editors and hints at a future where the generation is free and untethered. If multimodal labs commoditize creation, value migrates to orchestration layers (an arena where Adobe boasts decades of UX muscle but no guaranteed moat). The asymmetry between today’s cash flow and tomorrow’s uncertainty warrants a hold stance for patient investors.

Firefly as Subscription Adhesive

Adobe’s first priority in the generative era is to keep Creative Cloud the default hub for paid-for content production. Firefly actions are now metered through “generative credits,” but subscribers currently face no effective cap on standard image or vector tasks, reducing any incentive to experiment elsewhere. By bundling AI costs inside existing plans, Adobe turns what could have been a margin headwind into a user-experience nudge that deepens ARR.

Management reports that the Digital Media segment’s ARR rose to 18.09 billion dollars, up 12.1 percent from last year, showing that adding AI to existing subscriptions fuels growth rather than cannibalizing revenue. At the same time, Firefly’s promise of “commercially safe” outputs appeals to agencies concerned about the copyright pitfalls that often surround open-source models.

Even so, some users remain price conscious. Creators on Reddit often compare Firefly’s results with free options and wonder if a paid upgrade makes sense for personal projects. Those discussions hint that Adobe’s subscription strength could weaken as more advanced all-in-one AI tools appear.

Workflow Utility Vs. Free-Form Generation

Generative Extend in Premiere shows Adobe’s preference for practical tools over eye-catching tricks. Instead of making an entire new scene, it slips in a few smooth seconds to patch a drone clip or extend a reaction shot. This focus embeds AI into muscle memory, reducing churn even if headline-grabbing text-to-video tools run faster.

Google’s Veo 3 upends that balance by generating eight-second clips complete with native audio, available to any Ultra subscriber for $249 per month, a fraction of an annual Creative Cloud All Apps license. If clients accept Veo content for commercial spots, Adobe’s decades-old workflow advantage could erode.

The company counters by positioning Firefly as “model-agnostic middleware” that can absorb frontier weights as they stabilize, but integration roadmaps remain vague. Until Adobe proves it can snap in outside-foundation models without cannibalizing margin, investors will discount its omnivorous narrative.

Competitive Velocity

Veo 3’s viral demonstrations (ranging from AI-generated Will Smith pasta memes to short films scored with original dialogue) have reignited questions about where the real moat lies: in tools or in distribution. Adobe responded at Summit 2025 by expanding GenStudio, a supply-chain layer that marries Experience Cloud data with Creative Cloud content to automate asset personalization at scale.

While this top-of-funnel integration appeals to marketers, it assumes continued dominance in downstream creation. Open platforms like Canva have already siphoned casual users, and Veo’s browser-native approach could repeat that dynamic in video. The risk is that Adobe defends too many fronts (creative pros, marketing ops, and document workflows) while smaller rivals cherry-pick high-growth niches.

Regulatory scrutiny adds another variable: EU and U.S. bodies increasingly view AI-assisted SaaS suites as potential gatekeepers. Any mandated interoperability could accelerate model swapping and weaken Adobe’s walled-garden thesis.

Financials

Revenue in Q2 FY25 climbed to $5.87 billion, driven by 11% growth in Digital Media and 10% in Digital Experience. GAAP operating income reached $2.11 billion for a 36% margin, while non-GAAP margin came in at 45%, aligning with management’s long-term target.

Cash from operations totaled $2.19 billion, up 13% versus the prior-year quarter, and Adobe closed the period with $4.93 billion in cash against $6.17 billion of long-term debt. The balance sheet remains light, enabling continued buybacks; 8.6 million shares were repurchased in Q2, offsetting dilution from stock-based compensation.

Guidance now calls for FY25 revenue of $23.50–23.60 billion and GAAP EPS of $16.30–16.50, modestly above prior midpoints. At the midpoint, Adobe is guiding for roughly 12% top-line growth and 46% non-GAAP operating margin, implying continued cost discipline even as GPU-cluster expenses rise for Firefly training.

Segment detail shows Digital Media ARR expanding faster than revenue, suggesting seat growth plus price realization from AI features. Digital Experience subscription revenue grew 11%, a reminder that Experience Cloud’s data platform helps diversify away from purely creative budgets.

Risks

Generative commoditization is the most pressing threat. Should open-weight or big-tech models replicate Firefly quality at negligible cost, Creative Cloud’s price-value equation could compress quickly, pressuring both ARR and margin. Veo 3 illustrates how fast that scenario can materialize.

Second, it is still unclear when Adobe will begin charging for AI credits. For now, the company lets users access standard features without limits to encourage adoption, but once those caps take effect, customers may resist the extra fees, which could slow attachment rates.

Finally, macro-sensitive marketing budgets could stall Experience Cloud momentum. Management’s raised guidance assumes stable conditions; a sudden ad-spend retrenchment would flow through to subscription revenue and deferred backlog, magnifying operating leverage on the downside.

Conclusion

Adobe trades at 18.9 x forward earnings, 19.2 x EV/EBITDA, and 8.3 x sales. Autodesk commands roughly 29 x forward earnings and 42 x EV/EBITDA, while Microsoft sits near 36 x P/E and 25 x EV/EBITDA thanks to Azure’s scale. On sales multiples, Adobe’s 8 x is below its five-year average yet still above Salesforce’s 6.5 x, hinting that the market prices in more durability than disruption.

Our neutral stance flows from that valuation balance. Cash flow strength and entrenched workflows merit a premium to slower-growing SaaS peers, but the pace of generative innovation outside the Adobe stack means that premium may never return to previous highs. Investors already exposed to the name can comfortably hold, harvesting buybacks and incremental AI upsell, while new entrants may prefer to watch how Veo 3 reshapes customer expectations before assigning a higher multiple. In short, Adobe is fairly valued for today’s dominance yet vulnerable to tomorrow’s creative free market (an equilibrium that argues for patience rather than chase).

data.title: Oxford Industries Is Getting To More Interesting Valuations Despite Higher Risks
data.description:
Like most apparel retailers and brands, Oxford Industries' (NYSE:OXM) Q1'25 results were not good, with falling comp sales and deleveraging margins, added to traffic and tariff pressures.

To this, we have to add company-internal problems with capital allocation and brand management. Still, OXM has several quality characteristics and is approaching a low multiple of historical cycle-average earnings. I maintain a Hold, but am I getting more interested as the stock price decreases.

A typical retailer's Q1'25 results

OXM is among the last apparel retailers to report Q1 results (its Q1 goes from February to April). The results were very similar to other apparel retailers and brands in many aspects.

On the topline, comps were down 5%. This has some cyclical impacts from traffic and discretionary softness, but also brand-level differences. For example, Lilly Pulitzer was up 12%, coming from a strong FY24 and showing good brand search momentum, while both Tommy Bahama and Johnny Was posted mid- to high-single-digit declines. So, the same macro, and in some cases the same consumer demographic (LP and JW), generates very different results. The company has to work on brand, positioning, and above all, product.

On the gross margin side, the picture is also similar to peers. Gross margin declined 100 bps, mostly due to cyclical inventory cost normalization (freight and cotton costs were lower in the 2023/24 cycle) and increased promotional activity to support weaker traffic. In addition, OXM records outbound logistics (when the company sends a package to an ecomm customer) in CoGS, whereas most companies record that in SG&A. That said, the decline is relatively contained considering OXM still operates above 60% gross margin levels.

SG&A was the worst line in the P&L. As is typical, sales deleverage added pressure, but the company also increased SG&A in absolute terms, up 5% YoY. This was mainly due to new store openings and remodels. OXM added 30 new locations vs. Q1'24, with 8 net adds after closures, including several new Marlin Bar restaurant concepts. These investments added labor, occupancy, and pre-opening expenses to the cost base.

This last point may be the most questionable aspect of OXM’s strategy. The company is adding costs and fixed assets into a challenging macro environment, and also spending capital aggressively when liquidity is tightening. Management reiterated full-year plans to spend ~$125 million on a new distribution center in Georgia, while also maintaining a $40 million annual dividend and repurchasing $50 million of stock in Q1 alone. The company ended the quarter with just $4 million in cash (cyclically low due to spring/summer inventory build), and long-term debt rose to $118 million, up ~$100 million YoY. If the pace of investment continues, the company could add another $100 million of debt by year-end, going from net cash to a 4x net income debt profile. Not great.

Valuation

The company is an apparel brand and is therefore cyclical.

We need to consider not only near-term earnings (which were revised down again, from $6.70 EPS in FY24 to $4.50 guided originally for FY25 in Q4'24, and now to $2.80–$3.20) but long-term earning power as well. I believe OXM’s normalized mid-cycle earnings are around $60–70 million, or $4–5 per share.

At a market cap of ~$640 million and a share price of $42, the stock is starting to approach a more interesting valuation, with an emphasis on starting.

That said, I prefer to wait for much lower prices to benefit from the stock's natural cyclicality. If the U.S. enters a recession and discretionary spending falls further, OXM could re-rate lower, especially because the company has now added debt risk.

I maintain a Hold, but am I getting more interested in OXM at these levels.

data.title: Arena Group: From Turnaround To Growth Story
data.description:
My most successful strategy has been buying companies that have significantly improved without the market yet catching on. I refer to these as turnarounds. The problem is, I only find a handful of these each year. Arena Group Holdings, Inc. (AREN) is a prime example. A year ago, Arena was hemorrhaging money with excessive leverage, and a huge litigation liability. Bankruptcy appeared imminent. Today it’s solidly profitable. The turnaround is complete.

What investors are now missing is this is now a growth story in the new online media space. It appears to have a formula for significant sustainable growth. Yet, the stock trades for only 10x likely earnings. I believe this disconnect is the result of no analyst coverage, a NYSE American listing, and a history, until mid-last year, of large losses.

Background

Arena IPO’d in February 2022 on the NYSE American Exchange. It is an online platform of owned media sites and 150 independent sites that benefit from the advertising dollars and numerous services Arena brings in. The owned sites are Athlon Sports, Men’s Journal, TheStreet, Parade, Surfer, The Spun, and Parade Home & Garden among others. On May 12th, 2025 it added Travelhost for $1 million. The independent publishers incur their own costs to create content and benefit from Arena’s search engine optimization, social media, ad monetization, audience development, and subscription marketing. None of its content is AI written. Almost all revenues are from online publications. Arena still prints some magazines, but more for marketing purposes as it’s not a profit center.

The company used to publish Sports Illustrated under license from Authentic Brands. In early 2024, Arena ceased publication and was sued by Authentic for license payments and other costs. On April 29, 2025, Arena reached a settlement with Authentic Brands, which allowed them to remove all liability to Authentic. On that date, a $93.9 million balance sheet liability as of March 31, 2025, went away. The press release indicated it cost an immaterial amount to settle.

Manoj Bhargava owns 71.4% of the stock through Simplify Inventions, LLC. He served as the interim CEO from December 2023 to January 2024, and co-President February 2024 – April 2024. Mr. Bhargava is the Founder of Innovation Ventures LLC (5-hour ENERGY) and has served as its Chief Executive Officer since its inception. Mr. Bhargava founded 5-hour ENERGY in 2000 and has grown it to annual retail sales of over $1 billion.

Manoj invested $62 million into Arena in the past two years to keep it afloat, obtaining a majority stake. Paul Edmondson was named the new CEO in March 2025. Mr. Edmondson has been with the company since 2018. Four of the 6 directors resigned on April 29, 2025, and 1 new one was brought on. That currently leaves only 3 directors, who do not include Mr. Bhargava. The bylaws call for 3-6 directors. On June 6, 2025, it was announced that Arena had returned to compliance with all of the NYSE American’s continued listing standards. It had fallen out of compliance due to negative net worth and continued losses.

The business is somewhat seasonal with the first quarter being the slowest and the fourth being the busiest. The company currently does not do conference calls. However, in the last 2 quarters, it provided a video answering investor questions.

Financials

Arena lost $56 million in 2023 and $101 million in 2024. The majority of these losses were from now discontinued operations, namely Sports Illustrated. Discontinued operation losses were $121 million in those 2 years, or 77% of the total. That indicates even without Sports Illustrated, Arena was losing a lot of money. Financial results for the last 5 quarters are shown below.

Arena had been losing money for years prior to the third quarter of 2024. Everything changed that quarter due to a change in the business model, though these changes had been in the works for a while. With Sports Illustrated (their largest publication) gone, they were able to right-size the company. The executive ranks were reduced. Legal expenses have been significantly reduced. A large direct sales force was found to be unnecessary. The result was a reduction in staff from around 600 to currently around 160.

Both General & Administrative and Sales & Marketing costs have been cut in half and the gross margin jumped. To give you an idea of the scale of the operating expense cuts, in the most recent quarter, G&A declined year over year from $10.1 million to $5.3 million. S&M declined from $4.6 million to $2.1 million. The higher gross margin is due to changing from a large number of highly paid in-house writers to using more independent writers who are paid based on reader engagement. These reductions appear not only sustainable but there is capacity to grow without large new investment.

Earnings in the last 3 quarters since the major changes were made have totaled $0.33. The guidance for the current quarter indicates earnings of at least $0.12 or more. That puts the EPS current annual run rate at $0.45. However, as shown in the table above, prior earnings were understated due to software amortization exceeding software capex. When factoring that in, the annual EPS run rate is more like $0.50.

Revenue increased 10% last quarter year over year after factoring out Sports Illustrated. Revenues and earnings last quarter sequentially due to seasonality. The first quarter is the slowest and the fourth quarter the busiest. The guidance discussed below indicates growth is actually expected to significantly accelerate.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet has improved significantly over the past year but remains weak. Tangible net worth improved from -$224 million on June 30, 2024, to -$97 million as of March 31, 2025, after eliminating the litigation liability. Most of the rest of the improvement was capital raises from Manoj Bhargava and profits in the last 3 quarters. Currently, management’s primary use of cash flow is to pay down debt with a secondary use being small acquisitions. The $111 million of debt shown above comes due in December 2026. However, it is owed to a company controlled by Manoj Bhargava, a related party. Strong recent profits should lead to a refinancing at a significantly lower interest rate.

Guidance

Arena had not provided financial guidance in the past but did so for the first time last quarter. They guided for $42.5 million in revenues this quarter (2Q 2025), at the midpoint, and operating income of $10 million. The $10 million operating income is well above the $7.4 million in the first quarter of 2025. The revenue guidance is 56% above that realized in 2Q 2024. In the stockholder video about the quarter, Manoj stated he expects a $12 stock price by year-end.

Catalysts

This is no longer just a turnaround story, it’s increasingly a growth story too with a 56% revenue increase guide for this quarter. The catalysts to improve revenues, profits, and the stock price are listed below.

1. New Business Model - The primary catalyst is a change in the business model. The traditional media model was to find the best writers you could afford in order to put out the best product. Arena’s new model is actually more like Seeking Alpha. Writers are paid based on the number of readers they get. Arena goes beyond this by helping their independent writers businesses in other ways discussed earlier. The Sports Illustrated publication failed for Arena because readers were no longer willing to pay for the best writers. This new model was first applied to Arena’s now primary sports site, Athlon Sports.

Athlon’s audience traffic grew over 500% in Q1 2025 versus Q1 2024. Syndication and commerce revenue for Athlon grew 730% year over year versus Q1 2024. Website traffic to Athlon as measured by semrush.com is shown below.

Arena has now committed to bring its other owned sites to this new model.

Men’s Journal was recently converted to the new model. Its traffic increased 282% over the previous month to 33.1 million page views in March 2025, the first month the new business model was applied.

TheStreet also had record traffic levels in March 2025 with 80 million page views, up 100% versus March 2024.

2. Lower Interest Rates - There is a major opportunity to significantly reduce interest expense. Arena is currently paying about 11% on its debt, which is mostly to Manoj Bhargava’s company. That debt matures in December 2026. However, the debt was incurred when Arena wasn’t profitable. With 3 quarters of profits now, they should be able to refinance to a much lower interest rate. They may be able to reduce interest expense by at least $4 million per year. That would be a $0.02 improvement in EPS per quarter.

3. Bolt-on Acquisitions – The price of many legacy magazine publishers is dropping and many names with brand equity can now be acquired for little money. In May 2025, Arena acquired the venerable Travelhost brand for $1 million. They expect to apply their performance business model to it. Arena’s return to solid profitability will allow it to pursue more small bolt-on deals. Management has said they would like to do 4 of these smaller bolt-ons a year. These acquisitions are likely to be lower risk as they are mostly buying just the names with few other assets, liabilities or costs.

4. Performance Marketing – This revenue line item found in Note 17 (Segment Reporting) of the footnotes jumped to $4.7 million in the most recent quarter from $0.3 million a year earlier. This is marketing content prepared for commercial customers. Arena is paid based on consumer purchases done through that marketing content.

5. Syndicating Content – Arena has recently gotten significant new revenues syndicating content to third-party sites. This revenue has very high margins.

6. Low Capex – Arena can grow significantly without a lot of investment. It has relatively few fixed asset needs and little inventory to fund. Its main investment is in software. The platform they need to grow existing media properties and acquire new ones is already in place. Having the capacity to grow without significant investment usually leads to earnings growth exceeding revenue growth.

7. Up-listing – Arena would benefit from an up-listing to the NYSE or Nasdaq. This would add liquidity and exposure to its stock. It is currently listed on the NYSE American Exchange.

8. Tax Loss Carryforwards – Arena had $210 million of tax loss carryforwards as of December 31, 2024. That should be sufficient to shelter profits for many years to come.

9. AI Training Fees – Though not mentioned by Arena, this is a possible revenue source in the future. The New York Times, among others, have already done this. Curiosity Stream, a smaller company I used as a comparable in the Valuation section below, is doing this in a big way.

Concerns

Arena still has a large negative tangible net worth. After adjusting for the settlement with Authentic, it was -$96.7 million as of March 31, 2025.

Arena is primarily reliant on advertising for revenue. That could be impacted in a recession.

Four directors resigned in April 2025. These directors had been brought on board when Arena was struggling and left once stabilized.

Technicals

As shown in the chart below, AREN stock jumped from $0.57 to $1.75 on November 15th, 2024, when the first quarterly profit was reported for the third quarter of 2024. It had been trading at bankruptcy levels. It then jumped again to as high as $9.76 on May 19, 2025, after three major positive announcements. These were two more strong quarters and the announcement of the settlement of litigation with Authentic Brands. But then the stock backtracked by half to the $5.17 it is today. I believe some of this backtracking is technical. That is the traders moving on from what had been a hot stock.

Some may say that an 800% increase since last November means the easy money has been made. But it’s not about where it’s been but what the upside is from here. The stock move so far reflects going from imminent bankruptcy to profitable but average. But the prospects here are a lot better than that.

Valuation

My usual method of valuation is to compare the subject company to its peers and then make adjustments for things like growth, leverage, and profit margins. I wasn’t able to find enough good comparables, so I added a second valuation method.

Competitors listed in the last 10-K are; Vice, BuzzFeed, Business Insider, WordPress, Medium, RebelMouse, Arc, Leaf Group, Future PLC, Fortune, CNN, ESPN, Yahoo!, Google, YouTube, Twitter, Reddit, and Liberty Alliance. The last 8 are way larger and include other businesses and are not comparable for valuation purposes. Of the others, only BuzzFeed and Future PLC are publicly traded. BuzzFeed is not currently profitable, making it difficult to use for valuation purposes. Newsmax recently IPO’d, but there is insufficient history at this point.

Probably the best comparable is Curiosity Stream (CURI) which has a very similar recent history to Arena. Both were left for dead a year ago and changed their business model and cut costs to become profitable. Both stocks have strongly rallied and fallen back some. CURI is a video media company and is smaller. It trades for 5.0x estimated 2025 revenues. Future PLC trades for 1x estimated 2025 revenues. These are very far apart, but I believe this indicates the upside potential for CURI is much higher than Future PLC. Future PLC has had stagnant revenues. I believe Arena should be valued between the two. Its revenue growth prospects are actually above CURI, but that is offset for now by a weak balance sheet. Keep in mind they are guiding for a 56% revenue growth this quarter. Based on the two comparables, I put Arena’s value at 3.0x revenues, which look likely to exceed $150 million this year. That puts the market cap at $450 million which rounds to $9.50 per share.

For a second methodology, I compared Arena to its industry sector. The consumer discretionary sector (XLY) has an average PE ratio of 32 right now according to the Wall Street Journal. The stocks in the XLY are all much larger and mostly better capitalized than Arena. Arena does have similar if not better growth prospects. Discounting for size and capitalization, I get a PE of 20. That would indicate a stock price of $10. That is also below the average U.S. PE Ratio right now of 25, despite having at least as good of growth prospects as the average.

Splitting the two methods, my 1-year price target for Arena Group is $9.75, which is a bit below the $12 year-end guidance provided by Manoj Bhargava.

Takeaway

Arena was a stock priced for bankruptcy 7 months ago. It has since changed its business model, eliminated a huge litigation liability from its balance sheet, and reported 3 strongly profitable quarters. More catalysts are in process as the company moves its new business model to all of its owned brands, acquires cheap unwanted legacy magazines, and has the opportunity to significantly lower interest expenses. If the performance publishing model continues to work as well as it has in its first few months, my price target may need to be doubled.

I believe Arena is still undiscovered, due to no analyst coverage, a poor track record until last year, and not trading on the NYSE or Nasdaq. A recent rally has been cut in half as traders moved on after it stalled. This provides a window of opportunity for investors to get into a highly profitable, solid-growth online media company majority owned by a proven entrepreneur.
